title: Optimized protocol for brain and head kidney catalase activity in zebrafish
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"2aj81","text":"Zebrafish, and other small teleosts, are used as experimental models to evaluate human pathologies, including those linked to oxidative stress. The protocol presents an optimized technique to evaluate the activity of catalase, an important antioxidant enzyme, in zebrafish tissues, focusing on the brain and head kidney. The protocol is based on the classical Aebi (1984) method.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"egjjm","text":"This protocol is intended to analyze catalase activity in zebrafish brain and head kidney tissue samples. It can be adapted for other fish species or other tissues without much hassle; however, tissue amounts need to be adjusted.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"7vduo","text":"Every biochemical protocol needs to be validated in the laboratory when first introduced. The present protocol describes validation steps that were taken in LaNeC.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"cc7kv","text":"Make sure to read all Safety Data Sheets for the reagents. Hydrogen peroxide causes serious eye damage; is harmful if inhaled and may cause respiratory irritation; and causes skin irritation. Therefore, use personal protective equipment whenever manipulating it. Moreover, hydrogen peroxide must be manipulated under a fume hood at all times.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Prepare reagents for the linearity curve: 1. Prepare phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.3) 2. Dilute 0.34 ml 30% hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) with phosphate buffer to 100 ml, reaching a final concentration of 30 mM H 2 O 2 . 3. Make seven serial dilutions of 30 mM H 2 O 2 (down to 0.234375 mM) in phosphate buffer Phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.3) [80 ml 0.1 M Na2HPO4 (13.8 g Na2HPO4⋅H2O/liter dH2O) 20 ml 0.1 M NaH2PO4 (26.8 g NaH2PO4⋅7H2O/liter dH2O)] Contributed by users Hydrogen peroxide 30% Merck Millipore (EMD Millipore) Catalog #822287.1000 12x75 mm high clarity polypropylene test tubes Corning Catalog #352063

H 2 O 2 quantification 1. Allow reagents to equilibrate, and pour in a Quartz cuvette. 2. Read on a spectrophotometer at 240 nm. ATTENTION All analyses should be made with at least three technical replicates Thoroughly rinse the cuvettes with distilled water to remove contaminants before each replicate

Data should be entered in two columns, 'x' and 'y', representing concentration and response, respectively. Plot data and perform a linear regression: a a R 3.4.4 Xubuntu 16.04 https://www.r-project.org/ if(!require(chemCal)){ install.packages('chemCal') library(chemCal)}m Determine linearity and curve parameters using chemCal for R if(!require(chemCal)){ install.packages('chemCal') library(chemCal) } m <- lm(y ~ x, data = your_data) summary(m) calplot(m) Determine linearity and curve parameters using chemCal for R chemCal for R 0.2.1 Xubuntu 16.04 https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/chemCal/index.html Johannes Ranke (https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4371-6538)

The first step in determining the sensitivity of the assay is to determine the limit of detection (LOD). This can be done using parameters from the linear model, with the function 'lod' from the R package chemCal. Use alpha = 0.01 and beta = 0.5. chemCal for R 0.2.1 Xubuntu 16.04 https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/chemCal/index.html Johannes Ranke (https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4371-6538) lod(m, alpha = 0.01, beta = 0.5) Calculate limit of detection using chemCal for R

The next step in determining sensitivity of the assay is to determine the limit of quantification (LOQ). This can also be done using parameters from the linear model, with the 'loq' function from the package chemCal. Substitute 'your_n' by the number of technical replicates in the your assay. a loq(m, n = your_n) Determine limit of quantification with the chemCal package chemCal for R 0.2.1 Xubuntu 16.04 https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/chemCal/index.html Johannes Ranke (https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4371-6538)

Precision refers to the dispersion of measured values around an average value, and its numerical value is determined by the relative standard deviation (RSD): RSD = 100 * s / |x̄|Where:s = the sample standard deviationx̄ = sample mean

Intermediate precision, or inter-run precision, defines the precision of a measurement made in the same laboratory made by different analysts and/or different days. We follow ANVISA's recommendations by running three technical replicates for the lowest, intermediate, and highest H 2 O 2 concentrations in two different days and assessing the RSD for each concentration. RSDs higher than 15% indicate low intermediate precision.

Accuracy can be determined after the establishment of linearity, sensitivity, and precision. Run another calibration curve, with at least three technical replicates, for the entire range of concentrations. Accuracy is calculated as the difference between predicted (xi) and measured (x v ) absorbances Predicted values can be obtained based on the linearity curve (Step 1) using a weighted linear model, with weights derived from the curve made for the accuracy determination. Using chemCal (where new_data refers to the curve made for accuracy determination) chemCal for R 0.2.1 Xubuntu 16.04 https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/chemCal/index.html Johannes Ranke (https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4371-6538) weights Generate predicted values from weighted linear model (weights based on the results from the linearity assay) weights <- with(new_data, { yx <- split(y, x) ybar <- sapply(yx, mean) s <- round(sapply(yx, sd), digits = 2) w <- round(1 / (s^2), digits = 3) }) new_data.means <- aggregate(y ~ x, new_data, mean) n <- lm(y ~ x, w = weights, data = new_data.means) inverse.predict(new_data.means, 30, ws = your_weight) #your_weight determined in previous step for 30 μM inverse.predict(new_data.means, 15, ws = your_weight) #your_weight determined in previous step for 15 μM inverse.predict(new_data.means, 7.5, ws = your_weight) #your_weight determined in previous step for 7.5 μM inverse.predict(new_data.means, 3.75, ws = your_weight) #your_weight determined in previous step for 3.75 μM inverse.predict(new_data.means, 1.875, ws = your_weight) #your_weight determined in previous step for 1.875 μM inverse.predict(new_data.means, 0.9375, ws = your_weight) #your_weight determined in previous step for 0.9375 μM inverse.predict(new_data.means, 0.46875, ws = your_weight) #your_weight determined in previous step for 0.46875 μM inverse.predict(new_data.means, 0.234375, ws = your_weight) #your_weight determined in previous step for 0.234375 μM Generate predicted values from weighted linear model (weights based on the results from the linearity assay)
Use the predicted values x i obtained in the previous step to calculate the accuracy at each level, as: Accuracy = ([xi - x v ] / x v ) * 100
Sacrifice animal with ice-cold water (< 12 ºC) followed by spinal transection.
Carefully dissect the brain (http://dx.doi.org/10.3791/1717) and head kidney (http://dx.doi.org/10.3791/2839). http://dx.doi.org/10.3791/1717 http://dx.doi.org/10.3791/2839

Homogenize the tissue in 2 ml PB (0.1 M, pH 7.3) Phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.3) [80 ml 0.1 M Na2HPO4 (13.8 g Na2HPO4⋅H2O/liter dH2O) 20 ml 0.1 M NaH2PO4 (26.8 g NaH2PO4⋅7H2O/liter dH2O)] Contributed by users Tissues can be kept frozen (- 20 ºC) for up to 6 months.
Re-suspend the homogenate by vortexing, if needed

Correlation coefficients higher than 0.98 suggest linearity. Error residuals should also be checked, looking for deviations in linearity, presence of atypical samples, heteroscedasticity, and dependence between errors; a well-adjusted curve should present erros with a uniform distribution, average zero and constant variance, and absence of atypical samples. An F-test comparing fits of residuals with a linear vs. quadratic model can also be used.

The first step in determining the precision is to assess the repeatability of the assay (that is, intra-run precision). Run three technical replicates for the lowest, intermediate, and highest H 2 O 2 concentrations. Determine intra-run precision by calculating the RSD for each of these concentrations. RSDs higher than 15% indicate low precision.
Carefully transfer the homogenate to the cuvette.
Add 1 ml H 2 O 2 (30 mM) solution. Immediately record
